title: Breaking the Habit of Being Yourself

chapters.title: Break the habit of being yourself
chapters.text:
Your brain runs the show. It shapes how you think, feel, act, and connect with the people around you. It is the core of who you are, and it touches every choice you make. A new wave of science suggests that any of us can build a reality of our choosing with the power of the mind. Dr. Joe Dispenza draws on biology, genetics, brain chemistry, neuroscience, and quantum physics to show what is possible.

By changing your brain, you change your reality.

In Breaking the Habit of Being Yourself, you will pick up ways to move the needle in every area of your life. You will meet the idea of a quantum reality, a view of the physical world as an information field that is everything and nothing at the same time. A quantum field's existence means there is no single fixed reality. Many possible outcomes live in the field, and we can pull any of them into being. Picture yourself stepping into your new home, seeing every detail. By holding that image, you have pointed the field at one possible reality. With care, you can make it the main one. You are the maker of what surrounds you.

Dr. Dispenza bridges old wisdom and new science, building a link between the scientific and the spiritual. He has shared this work with thousands of people in twenty five countries, helping them rebuild their lives from the inside out.

As Dispenza has put it, we should never wait for science to give us permission to do the uncommon. If we do, we turn science into another religion.

chapters.title: The brain runs your life
chapters.text:
The latest research on the brain, the quantum field, and consciousness opens new doors for personal growth and for tapping into our full potential. We now know that the human mind can build a new reality and even help the body move past illnesses that once seemed incurable. This guide shows how to bring these discoveries into everyday life.

Swap old habits for new ones, and you rewire your brain.

What you think and how you think it shapes the world around you. You can choose what to think. By directing your thoughts, you tap into the power to influence the world outside. Shift your mindset and your usual way of looking at things, and a new self and a new reality start to take shape. The real power sits in your attitudes and beliefs. You are what you believe.

Your environment shapes what you see as true. Society, friends, family, and the genes you carry set the frame for your guiding ideas.

The old way of being, the one that puts society in the lead role, needs an update. The time has come for change and for a new way of thinking. It is time for a new reality that fits your own needs.

It is hard to break the common stereotypes. Stereotypes make life easier because they spare us the need to choose. The brain does not have to think. But that kind of thinking cuts off the brain's ability to lift our lives and our health. Stepping toward change means leaving your comfort zone. Is the ability to shape the quality of your life worth the effort? You can change your life by changing your usual way of seeing. Keep going until you hit your goals, and put this knowledge to work for your own growth.

chapters.title: Focused thought can shape matter
chapters.text:
To shift the normal state of things, you have to believe there is something out there beyond your current reality. Plant the thought in your mind that it is possible. There is an infinite number of possible realities waiting to be born. Your brain has the power to bring one of them to life.

The observer effect is one way to pull the life you have been dreaming about into being. To grasp it, you need to know that the world is built from countless tiny pieces, like electrons. They exist as possibilities until an observer notices them. They can be everything and nothing at the same time. The moment the observer looks closely, all those probabilities lock in.

When observers look at an electron, they pull it into reality. The finding confirms a link between mind and matter. Your energy flows where your focus goes. Put another way, when you think about something good, you are aiming your energy at making that joyful event real. In quantum theory, all realities exist at once. There is a reality in which you land the new job, and one in which you do not. You get to pick the one you prefer.

Our thoughts and feelings choose our reality. Think in a new way, and your life can change.

In the quantum field, there is already a reality in which you are happy, healthy, and driving that dream car. Your mind can be the observer that locks the electrons in place. The reality you dream of is waiting for you to notice it. Our thoughts and feelings link us to the field. Wanting something and setting a goal is not enough. You have to match your aims and your emotional reactions to the reality you want. When you feel clear passion and a deep sense of delight, the field reads that state as a request. The field responds to who you are, not to what you want.

Attention is the key that makes your thoughts and ideas real.

The subconscious can pull from unlimited energy. Consciousness and energy are tightly linked.

Each of us can shape the world around us. Old thinking said reality was fixed. New knowledge of the quantum universe pushes back on that. We are part of an invisible field of energy with infinite possibilities. We can act on that field with our thoughts and feelings.

chapters.title: Keep thinking the old way and you keep the old reality
chapters.text:
Do the same things every day, and you build the same reality. Old habits, old thoughts, and old memories are wired deep in our minds, and they call the shots. They tell us who to be and how to feel. Wake up in the morning, look around, and the familiar things tell you who you are. The blue toothbrush, the floral print coffee cup, the same bus to work. You run on memory, sometimes without noticing.

You see the same faces at work, exchange the same small talk, and tick off the same tasks. You go to the same store for milk, and the next morning you drink from the same floral cup. The loop runs day after day. Plant the same seed and you cannot be surprised when the same plant comes up.

You define yourself through habits and past experience.

Remembering and replaying past events are brain jobs. The brain stores a record of every event you have lived through. When something close to it happens again, you react based on that stored record. The cells that make up your thinking carry a reflection of your whole life.

Repeat the same thoughts and actions, and you build patterns so tight they run on their own. It gets hard to see or question them. Through that template, the brain pins the reality it has built onto the quantum field as the only one that is real.

To break out of that closed loop, you have to change who you are. And for that, you have to think differently. The power of thought is strong. When you focus on something and picture it, the brain treats it like a real event. When you act and feel as if your dream has already come true, you set the body up to make it real. You prepare and rewire yourself to step into a new reality. First, you change. Then, your reality changes.

chapters.title: A habit is the body taking over the mind
chapters.text:
About 95 percent of our reactions are automatic, which means almost everything we do is unconscious. We run on scripts, like machines running a program. We do not build a new reality. We replay learned patterns. Walking, reading, blaming others, doubting ourselves, all of it can fall into that automatic loop. It helps to remember that thoughts belong to the mind, and feelings belong to the body.

The subconscious is a library of both physical and mental habits that happen below the level of awareness.

When we fuse with our emotions, we let the body run the mind. Picture this. Yesterday, you argued with a stranger on the street. Today, you remember the moment and think, I was so angry. You replay the feeling, and each pass makes the anger stronger. Feelings and thoughts merge, and the body takes the wheel.

You can even forget why you were angry because the mind's voice goes quiet when the body drives. Or take another example. You think of yourself as someone who always disappoints people. You may not even remember why you picked up that belief, but it has been with you for years. In that time, the body has learned to release chemicals that match the story. The body treats that state as normal, even familiar, and keeps replaying it. It is a bit like living in constant stress. Get used to it, and you start to depend on it. Even if someone tells you that you do not actually disappoint others, you may not believe it. The body will pull you back to the old reaction because it thinks that is what you need.

To build a new you, you have to know who you are now.

The first step in taking back control of your mind is to spot your old habits and attitudes. The self observation stage is where you consciously meet your old self.

chapters.title: The brain rewires itself with practice
chapters.text:
The brain is not a fixed object. It is a system that keeps reshaping itself as you use it. Dispenza walks the reader through the idea of neuroplasticity, the fact that the mind can grow new wiring with practice. This is the science behind the rest of the book.

He points to a few real examples. London cab drivers, who must memorize the city's maze of streets, grow a larger hippocampus, the part of the brain tied to memory. Musicians, who practice for years, develop a thicker corpus callosum, the bridge between the two halves of the brain. Even older adults, when they take up a new skill, show new growth in the white matter of the brain. The lesson is clear. The brain you have today is not the brain you have to keep.

The three parts of the brain, the neocortex for thought, the limbic system for feeling, and the cerebellum for habit, work as a team. When the team is stuck in old patterns, change feels hard. When the team is in sync, change feels like flow.

Dispenza draws on recent advances in quantum physics and the study of consciousness to support his case. He is careful to point out that the science is still young. Not every claim in the book has been proven in a lab. But the direction is clear. The mind is more flexible than we used to think.

The final piece is the role of attention. Where attention goes, energy flows. The thoughts you feed grow stronger. The thoughts you starve wither away. This is the daily practice that lets the new self take root.

The habit of being yourself is not a life sentence. It is a habit. And habits can be broken.

chapters.title: The habit of being yourself
chapters.text:
The title of the book is a clue. To break the habit of being yourself, you have to first see what that self looks like. Dispenza asks the reader to spend a week observing the self without judgment. Notice the body, the breath, the mood. Notice the loop of familiar thoughts. You are not trying to change anything yet. You are just looking.

The practice has ancient roots. Monks and mystics have long used observation of the inner self as a way to step outside the loop of automatic thought. The modern twist is that science now backs it up. Brain scans show that focused attention can shift which circuits are active in the mind.

The journey from the old self to a new one is not a single leap. It is a series of small steps, repeated until they grow into a new way of being. Each day you sit in meditation, each time you feel a hard feeling and let it pass, you are building the muscle.

Change is not about willpower. It is about showing up, again and again, until the new pattern becomes the default.

The mind, like the body, responds to practice. A pianist does not master a piece in one sitting. A runner does not run a marathon on day one. The same is true for the inner life. Patience is not passive. It is the active choice to keep going.

A closing note.

The path of change is open to anyone willing to do the work. The book ends with a simple challenge. Pick one small practice and commit to it for forty days. Watch what shifts. The new self is closer than you think.

chapters.title: Closing thoughts
chapters.text:
New findings about the quantum field, electrons, and energy open up possibilities for the average person. By understanding how the body works and how the brain runs, we can make our lives richer and more joyful. The brain shields us from sudden change. The body builds automatic habits and a comfort zone. But change lets us see what was hidden before. It shows familiar things from new angles.

Big changes in our lives, our relationships, and our bodies come from small shifts in how we use the brain. The ability to influence matter, situations, and mindsets, even without direct contact with the outside, points to the deep reach of human thought.

You can learn about the great potential of the human brain and its striking abilities at any age. No matter where you are or what you do, you can change yourself and your reality.

A small step to try.

Body part induction. This is the simple act of feeling the location of your body, with the mind's help. Try to feel and notice the position of each part of your body. The practice helps the brain settle and find balance.

Water rising practice. Sit down and surrender your body. Dr. Dispenza means picturing water flowing into the room as you sit, rising slowly until it covers the room and your body.

Guided meditation. Meditating with paced breathing is the goal of guided meditation.

If you have read this far, the work has already started.
